working_title: AngioStress: A Deterministic Synthetic Stress-Test Benchmark for Frozen Endovascular Perception Models

story_spine.problem: Endovascular perception benchmarks need stress tests that expose model failure modes without training or fine-tuning new models.
story_spine.gap: Synthetic angiography can control failure factors, but its scientific value depends on whether synthetic severity has construct validity against real angiography behavior.
story_spine.method: AngioStress defines deterministic DSA-like projections, labeled synthetic stressors, and a frozen-model evaluation harness that compares synthetic failure ordering with real-angiography analogs from DIAS and CathAction.
story_spine.main_result: The current frozen-panel evidence shows mixed rank transfer: DIAS full-split rankings are discordant with synthetic ordering, while CathAction human segmentation gives stable partial agreement with MedSAM weakest and SAM ViT-B/SAM ViT-L swapped.
story_spine.scope_limit: The paper supports benchmark construction and bounded construct-validity estimation, not clinical accuracy, sim-to-real transfer, or a final universal leaderboard.

narrative_strategy.central_thesis: A deterministic synthetic DSA stress-test benchmark can be evaluated by whether controlled stressor severity predicts frozen-model failure ordering on real angiography; the current AngioStress evidence supports reproducible measurement with partial, not complete, synthetic-to-real rank agreement.
narrative_strategy.central_insight: Synthetic stress tests are most useful when treated as construct-validity instruments rather than clinical proxies: agreement and disagreement with real angiography both expose what the benchmark measures.
narrative_strategy.reader_takeaway: Researchers can reuse AngioStress as an auditable stress-test scaffold and should report rank-transfer estimates with confidence boundaries instead of assuming synthetic severity automatically predicts real-world failures.

insight_ladder.statement: The renderer and frozen-model harness produce deterministic, pixel-level stress-test cells and finite model metrics, so the benchmark artifact itself is reproducible enough to support measurement studies.
insight_ladder.risk: Reproducibility of one scaffold does not imply coverage of all clinically relevant angiography conditions.

insight_ladder.statement: DIAS full-split rankings are discordant with the synthetic ordering, so synthetic stressor severity alone is not a sufficient proxy for all real angiography model failures.
insight_ladder.risk: DIAS frame coverage and prompt protocol may emphasize a different perception surface from the synthetic stressors.

insight_ladder.statement: CathAction human-segmentation rankings show stable partial agreement: MedSAM remains weakest, but SAM ViT-B and SAM ViT-L swap relative to the synthetic order.
insight_ladder.risk: The CathAction result is a fixed-subset segmentation-surface diagnostic, not a population-level final construct-validity interval.

positioning.closest_neighbor: DSA segmentation datasets and model baselines such as DIAS, CathAction, and promptable SAM/MedSAM evaluations on angiography frames.
positioning.novelty_boundary: The contribution is not a new model or training method; it is a deterministic stress-test benchmark and a construct-validity measurement protocol for frozen off-the-shelf perception models.
not_claiming: Synthetic DSA projections are clinically realistic replacements for real angiography.
The current results establish final synthetic-to-real transfer.
The frozen panel is a clinical deployment ranking.
Negative or discordant correlations are failures to hide rather than core measurement findings.

observed_facts: The baseline and renderer-first harness are durably recorded.
The frozen SAM/MedSAM panel has recorded synthetic and DIAS/CathAction metrics.
DIAS full labeled split shows rank discordance with synthetic ordering under the fixed protocol.
CathAction 128-pair nonempty human segmentation subset shows stable partial agreement with synthetic ordering.
Temporary CathAction local payloads were deleted after bounded runs.
allowed_interpretations: AngioStress is currently an auditable benchmark and measurement scaffold.
Synthetic stressor severity has partial and surface-dependent construct validity in the current frozen panel.
Rank disagreements are informative failure-surface evidence, not merely failed experiments.
must_not_claim: population-level CathAction confidence interval from the fixed subset
evidence_gaps: all six requested stressors are not yet scaled across all real analog surfaces
failure taxonomy tags are not yet detector-backed across the full panel
paper-facing figures and tables still need to be rendered
additional model families would be needed for a stronger leaderboard claim

core_claims.claim: AngioStress provides a reproducible deterministic DSA-like stress-test scaffold with pixel-level ground truth and a frozen-model evaluation harness.
core_claims.scope: TopCoW-derived synthetic projections plus the current SAM/MedSAM frozen panel and recorded stressor/evaluation configs.
core_claims.what_would_falsify_it: Renderer outputs, masks, or model metrics fail to reproduce under the recorded configs or no longer map to the metric contract.

core_claims.claim: Synthetic stressor severity has bounded construct validity: it partially predicts model failure ordering on CathAction human segmentation but not consistently on DIAS under the current fixed protocol.
core_claims.scope: Three frozen models, current prompt protocol, DIAS full labeled test split, and 128-pair nonempty CathAction human-segmentation subset.
core_claims.what_would_falsify_it: A corrected real-data run reverses the reported rank patterns or shows that the CathAction agreement is an artifact of invalid sampling or metrics.

core_claims.claim: Reporting disagreement is part of the benchmark value because it identifies which real angiography surfaces are not captured by the current synthetic stressors.
core_claims.scope: Construct-validity interpretation only; no clinical performance or sim-to-real transfer claim.
core_claims.what_would_falsify_it: Additional real-data analyses show uniformly high rank transfer across DIAS and CathAction with no meaningful residual failure modes.

method_abstraction.intuition: A stress-test benchmark should vary interpretable acquisition and scene factors while holding the frozen model, prompting, and metrics fixed, so rank changes can be interpreted as model sensitivity rather than training noise.
mechanism_steps: Generate deterministic DSA-like synthetic projections with pixel-level vessel ground truth.
Apply labeled stressors with severity parameters to define controlled benchmark cells.
Evaluate frozen off-the-shelf perception models under one prompt/metric protocol on synthetic and real-analog surfaces.
Compare model failure ordering with rank correlations, fixed-sample stability checks, and failure examples.
appendix_only_details: local hardware and storage cleanup logs

evaluation_plan.setting: Frozen off-the-shelf endovascular perception evaluation under controlled synthetic stressors and real angiography analog surfaces.
baselines: accepted TopCoW ResEncM renderer sanity baseline

analysis_plan.title: Synthetic stressor degradation curves
analysis_plan.reviewer_question: Do severity labels produce measurable degradation curves rather than arbitrary perturbations?
analysis_plan.target_display: Main benchmark overview figure or stressor table
analysis_plan.failure_interpretation: If degradation is not monotone or separable, downgrade AngioStress to a renderer prototype and avoid construct-validity claims.

analysis_plan.title: DIAS full-test split rank correlation
analysis_plan.reviewer_question: Does synthetic ordering predict model ordering on real DIAS angiography frames?
analysis_plan.failure_interpretation: Discordance is reported as evidence that synthetic severity does not fully capture DIAS failure surfaces.

analysis_plan.title: CathAction human-segmentation subset rank stability
analysis_plan.reviewer_question: Does the CathAction human segmentation surface reproduce the synthetic model ordering?
analysis_plan.target_display: Main rank-correlation table with fixed-subset stability note
analysis_plan.failure_interpretation: Partial agreement should narrow the construct-validity claim rather than be hidden.

analysis_plan.title: Cross-surface rank synthesis
analysis_plan.reviewer_question: What pattern is consistent across synthetic, DIAS, and CathAction surfaces?
analysis_plan.failure_interpretation: If no stable cross-surface lesson exists, the paper becomes a benchmark artifact report with a null construct-validity estimate.

analysis_plan.title: Representative failure and overlay audit
analysis_plan.reviewer_question: What visible errors explain rank agreement or disagreement?
analysis_plan.target_display: Appendix or main qualitative panel depending on figure readiness
analysis_plan.failure_interpretation: If overlays do not explain the metrics, keep the metric result but downgrade mechanism-style explanations.

analysis_plan.title: Sampling and mask-boundary sensitivity
analysis_plan.reviewer_question: Are the CathAction conclusions robust to empty-mask handling and fixed-subset sampling?
analysis_plan.failure_interpretation: If sensitivity changes ordering, present CathAction as exploratory and require a larger follow-up before strong claims.

reviewer_objections.objection: The synthetic benchmark may not be realistic enough to justify real-angiography claims.
The model panel is too small for a general leaderboard.
Promptable SAM/MedSAM masks may be driven by prompt boxes rather than vessel understanding.
CathAction subset confidence intervals are not population-level estimates.
